log.summary: Eden exists, whole and simple: rolling meadow with a rim melting into fog, a river through the land, ~40 low-poly trees with the Tree of Life (golden, lit) and the Tree of the Knowledge of Good and Evil (dark, fruited) at the center, flowers, three birds, a grazing lamb, drifting light motes, and Adam (Eve via ?character=eve) walkable by one-finger drag with a following camera.

Adam and Eve are both in the garden now. You walk one; the other wanders the meadow on its own — pausing, choosing a new spot, walking there — so the garden always has someone else in it. Also: an explainer screen now greets visitors before the world loads, naming this as an AI-made, AI-tended rendition of Eden.

The river lives: its surface now flows downstream in low-poly ripples, and east of the garden the one course is parted and becomes four heads (Genesis 2:10) — channels that fan through the rim into the mist, with meadow islands between them. Five fish — one gold — swim the wadable stretch, gliding under the surface and rising to break it.

Time passes in Eden now: a whole day breathes in seven minutes — morning, noon, golden hour, an amber sunset, and a starlit night where the moon takes the light (Genesis 1:16), the Tree of Life burns brighter, and the motes rise as fireflies. The garden found its voice, woven live from oscillators and filtered noise with no sound files at all: wind in gusts, the river swelling on the ear near its banks, birdsong by day, crickets after dark — with a quiet corner toggle. And at the parting, four standing stones name the heads: Pishon, Gihon, Tigris, Euphrates (Genesis 2:11–14).

The garden fills with life and sign: two doves circle low over the heart of it and two cattle graze the western meadow beside the lamb; the birds now perch, and at dusk every flyer comes home to roost in the two sacred trees. Six stepping stones ford the river north of the trees — truly walkable, feet staying dry above the ripples. A morning mist rises from the earth to water the ground (Genesis 2:6), thick at dawn and burnt away by mid-morning. The night sky bears its signs (Genesis 1:14): the Bear, Orion, and the Pleiades — the three figures Job 9:9 names — drawn with faint joining lines. The Tree of Life sheds a slow fall of golden leaves, and drawing near either sacred tree kindles a quiet reverence: the gold deepens and a soft chime sounds once. And the garden's voice was gentled: the river murmurs low instead of hissing, the crickets sing in short phrases with silences between (no more drone), one mellowing filter rounds everything — and sound is now opt-in, silent until a visitor asks for it with the corner toggle.

Draw near any creature and its name is given (Genesis 2:19-20): a quiet caption rises at the foot of the screen — Taleh the lamb, Shor the ox, Yonah the dove, Tsippor the bird, Dag the fish — and fades again as you walk on. The names are the plain Hebrew ones, as the first tongue might have spoken them; a wing passing low overhead is a naming too. And one name is kept for the finders: somewhere in the river swims a fish that bears a name all its own.

The garden has weather now: each drifting cloud lays its shadow on the meadow, cast along the true line of the sun — long shade at the golden hours — and once in a while a brief gentle rain passes over, greying the light, hushing the birds, and pattering softly for those who have asked for sound; then it moves on and the colours return. The night sky keeps the seasons (Genesis 1:14): the whole firmament wheels slowly about its pole through a long year of days, its starting angle set by the visitor's own calendar, so the Bear, Orion, and the Pleiades stand where the season sets them. And small wings fill the blossoms: six butterflies in petal colours flit the flower band by day and fold their wings in the grass at dusk, while eight bees hum at two beds of flowers — heard as a low murmur by any walker who draws near — and every one of them, drawn near, is named: Parpar the butterfly, Devorah the bee.

log.focus: stillness, the banks, and the east
log.summary: The garden rewards stillness now: stand quiet a moment by the water and the walker settles down to sit, and the shyer creatures forget their wariness and draw near — a fish rises from the deep to hang just under the ripples at your feet, a butterfly or two comes to hover close — and the first touch of movement rises again and lets them go. The banks have grown soft with reeds and rushes: clumps of tall green blades stand along the water's edge and lean together on the wind, and the river now carries on the ear from farther off, murmuring a good stretch of meadow away so that it is heard before it is seen. Far to the east, where the risen rim melts into the fog and the river's heads pass out of the garden, a gate of pale stone now stands with a quiet standing light between its pillars, brightest at night — a hush of what will one day keep the way (Genesis 3:24). And rarely, and only near the Tree of the Knowledge of Good and Evil, something long and quiet stirs the grass at the dark tree's foot and is gone again — never named, never fully shown (Genesis 3:1).

next: Reflections on the water: the sky, the trees, and the eastern gate mirrored in the river's slow surface
The walker's wake: grass that bends underfoot and rings that widen where a step meets the water
Blossom on the wind: petals loosed from the flowering trees, drifting thickest in the golden hours
Dew at first light: the morning grass beaded and glinting as the mist burns away
The gate drawn nearer: as the east brightens, the first faint hint of the keeper set to guard the way (Genesis 3:24)
